script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Thursday — here is your QQQM recap. The portfolio closed one point five six percent down on the session. It was a broadly difficult day for technology and semiconductor names, with only a handful of holdings finding footing in positive territory.

Nvidia surged more than four percent while nearly everything else in the semiconductor complex sold off hard. Intel fell nearly ten percent. Advanced Micro Devices dropped more than six. The divergence inside one sector tells you almost everything about Thursday's session.

The Nasdaq Composite finished the session in negative territory, dragged lower by broad weakness across semiconductor and consumer discretionary names. No major macro data prints were released Thursday, though comments from Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh continued to circulate in markets, with analysts parsing language around the rate path for the back half of the year. The weight of that uncertainty sat squarely on high-multiple technology stocks throughout the session.

The lead story Thursday belongs to Intel, and it is not a good one. Intel closed down nine point seven nine percent, settling at one hundred ten dollars and twenty-four cents — the steepest single-session loss among all primary holdings. That move places Intel in sharp underperformance not just relative to the broader market, but relative to every other name in this portfolio. While no company-specific headline directly triggered the sell-off, the pressure on Intel reflects compounding structural concerns: the company continues to lose ground in both the data center processor market and the foundry business, and with Nvidia surging on artificial intelligence infrastructure demand, the contrast could not be more stark. Intel's decline also occurred in a session where Applied Materials and Lam Research — both critical to semiconductor fabrication — sold off between three and five percent, suggesting the pressure extended across legacy chipmakers and equipment suppliers alike. For holders of this portfolio, Intel at two point nine percent weight is the smallest primary position, but Thursday's move will not go unnoticed.

Now the full holdings rundown, starting with the largest positions.

Nvidia closed at two hundred four dollars and twelve cents, up eight dollars and fifty-seven cents, a gain of four point three eight percent on the session. That move stands in stark contrast to the broader semiconductor sector. The catalyst: Broadcom extended its chip supply agreement with Apple through twenty thirty-one, a deal reported at thirty billion dollars, and the news lifted sentiment across select artificial intelligence infrastructure names. Nvidia, as the dominant force in artificial intelligence accelerator chips, caught a bid as investors rotated toward names with durable demand visibility. Finance dot yahoo dot com noted that many high-flying artificial intelligence stocks have pulled into correction territory — but Nvidia was the clear exception Thursday. Watch Nvidia closely into earnings season; expectations remain elevated.

Apple closed at three hundred thirteen dollars and thirty-nine cents, up seventy-three cents, a gain of zero point two three percent. The Broadcom-Apple supply agreement was the defining Apple headline Thursday, with reports confirming the thirty-billion-dollar chip deal locked in through twenty thirty-one. That relationship reinforces Apple's long-term hardware supply chain integrity. Separately, research firm Omdia reported that worldwide personal computer shipments declined three point six percent year over year in the second quarter of twenty twenty-six, to sixty-five point seven million units. Notebook shipments fell four point two percent. Apple managed a modest gain despite that backdrop. Watch Apple heading into its own earnings report for any update on Mac demand trends.

Turning to Alphabet, which closed at three hundred sixty-one dollars and ninety-two cents, down four dollars and fifty-four cents, a loss of one point two four percent. Coverage from multiple outlets this week placed Alphabet in the context of the coming earnings season, with analysts flagging it as one of the names under meaningful scrutiny as the second quarter reporting period begins. The broader question for Alphabet is whether its artificial intelligence investments are translating into measurable revenue. The earnings setup will be the next major test.

Over at Micron Technology, the session was painful. Micron closed at nine hundred forty-eight dollars and eighty cents, down thirty-five dollars and ninety-five cents — a decline of three point six five percent. Analyst coverage Thursday highlighted the competitive positioning debate between Micron and South Korean rival SK Hynix, with SK Hynix preparing a Nasdaq listing via American depositary receipts. Futurum Equities' chief market strategist Shay Boloor was cited as preferring Micron, pointing to structural instability in Korean markets as a deterrent to the SK Hynix listing. Still, Thursday's price action reflected broader memory sector pressure. Watch the SK Hynix ADR debut Friday for any read-through to Micron sentiment.

Microsoft closed at three hundred eighty-three dollars and thirty-four cents, down three dollars and forty cents, a decline of zero point eight eight percent. No company-specific news drove the move. Microsoft tracked the broader technology sector lower, with its cloud and artificial intelligence exposure unable to insulate it from the session's selling pressure. The stock remains one of the more defensively positioned names in this portfolio given its diversified revenue base. Earnings season will be the next true catalyst.

Amazon closed at two hundred forty-three dollars and sixty-two cents, down fifty-four cents, a loss of zero point two two percent. The decline was minimal and Amazon was among the more resilient names in Thursday's session. Zacks Investment Ideas flagged Amazon alongside Alphabet and Nvidia in a note on artificial intelligence infrastructure positioning, reflecting continued analyst interest in Amazon Web Services as a core growth driver. The modest pullback looks more like noise than signal. Watch cloud revenue commentary when Amazon reports.

Meanwhile, Advanced Micro Devices had a difficult Thursday. The stock closed at five hundred seventeen dollars and forty cents, down thirty-four dollars and sixty-four cents — a drop of six point two eight percent. No single headline drove that move, but Advanced Micro Devices is directly exposed to the same artificial intelligence chip demand narrative as Nvidia, and on days when sentiment rotates selectively, it tends to see amplified moves in either direction. Thursday was the downside version of that trade. Upcoming earnings will be critical for any update on data center graphics processing unit market share.

Tesla closed at three hundred ninety-four dollars and six cents, down twenty-five dollars and seventy-one cents, a decline of six point one two percent. Finance dot yahoo dot com noted that Tesla investors will need patience on the robotaxi rollout, though better-than-expected delivery numbers have reinforced the underlying investment thesis for some analysts. The robotaxi timeline remains the central variable. Any update to commercial launch dates will move this stock.

Intel, as noted at the top, closed at one hundred ten dollars and twenty-four cents, down eleven dollars and ninety-six cents — a loss of nine point seven nine percent. The steepest decline in the primary holdings on Thursday. The structural headwinds facing Intel in both central processing unit competition and foundry execution are well documented. Thursday's session added another difficult data point to that record.

Three things to watch in the next twenty-four to forty-eight hours. First, the SK Hynix American depositary receipt debut on Friday — the offering of seventeen point seven nine million ADRs will test institutional appetite for memory sector exposure and may directly influence Micron Technology sentiment. Second, any further commentary from Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh on the interest rate path; analysts are actively reading his public remarks for forward guidance signals that could reprice high-multiple technology names. Third, the informal start of second-quarter earnings season — several large-cap technology companies are approaching their reporting windows, and pre-announcement risk or guidance updates could move positions across this entire portfolio.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch the SK Hynix debut closely — its reception in the market will set the tone for memory sector sentiment heading into the weekend.
